Model RMSE MAE Adj R²

Swap: LOSLVL + AGY + EDLVL² 21.58 16.61 0.3897

About This Model

What was done

Six models were estimated on federal employment salary data and evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation (3 repeats). The Stepwise BIC model was selected via bidirectional stepwise regression penalised by k = log(n). The remaining rows test whether swapping individual predictors improves out-of-sample performance over the simple three-variable baseline.

Reading the table

RMSE penalises large errors heavily — lower is better. MAE gives the average absolute miss in thousands of dollars. Adj R² is the share of salary variance explained out-of-sample. The green row is the best-performing model by RMSE.

What the swaps tell us

Each swap isolates one modelling choice — replacing raw years of service with an ordinal level, substituting agency identity for age level, or allowing non-linear returns to education. A swap that meaningfully lowers RMSE suggests that encoding carries genuine signal beyond what the baseline captures.

Residual Analysis

Row

Residual Analysis — Residuals (Actual minus Predicted) reveal where the model succeeds and where it struggles. Ideally residuals are centred at zero with symmetric spread and no systematic patterns across agencies or salary levels.

Row

Residual Distribution

Predicted vs. Actual

Row

Residual Distribution — Interpretation

📊

Residuals are roughly centred at zero as expected from OLS. The mild right skew is typical for salary data — a small number of very high earners are harder to predict, pulling the distribution rightward. Within DoD, senior civilian roles in components like OSD and DISA tend to sit in this right tail, as their compensation reflects specialised technical or policy responsibilities that the administrative predictors alone do not fully capture. A log transformation of SALARY may reduce this skew in future iterations.

Predicted vs. Actual — Interpretation

🎯

Points cluster along the 45° line confirming the model captures the central salary trend well. The spread widens at higher salaries — a classic sign of heteroscedasticity. This is especially visible for Army, Navy, and Air Force civilian employees in senior grades, where individual pay setting, locality adjustments, and retention incentives create variance that a single regression line cannot fully explain. Color-coding by agency reveals that no single branch dominates the misprediction — the spread is shared across components, suggesting the issue is structural rather than agency-specific.

Row

Residuals by Agency

Row

Residuals by Agency — Interpretation

🏛️

Each violin shows how prediction errors are distributed within a DoD component. Agencies whose violins sit above zero are systematically under-predicted — the model assigns them lower salaries than they actually earn. This often reflects mission-specific pay premiums: the Navy (NV) and Air Force (AF) civilian workforces include large concentrations of engineers and acquisition professionals whose salaries outpace what education level and seniority alone would predict. Agencies below zero are over-predicted, which can indicate a higher share of administrative or support roles paid below the grade-based expectation. Wide violins signal high within-agency variance — the Army (AR) in particular spans a broad range of occupational series from installation support to research science, making a single predicted value a poor fit for many individuals. Narrower violins, by contrast, suggest a more homogeneous workforce where the model performs consistently.

Salary Patterns

Row

Salary Patterns — Exploring how salary varies with length of service, agency, and education level, and how well the model captures those patterns across the Army, Navy, Air Force, and broader Defense components.

Row

Salary vs. Length of Service

Row

Length of Service — Interpretation

📈

Each line shows the agency-specific salary growth rate with seniority. Steeper slopes indicate a stronger seniority pay premium within that component. The Air Force (AF) and Navy (NV) civilian workforces tend to show steeper trajectories, reflecting their higher concentrations of technical and acquisition professionals who advance into well-compensated senior grades over time. The Army (AR) slope is comparatively flatter, consistent with a broader occupational mix that includes large numbers of lower-graded support and administrative roles alongside its technical workforce. The diverging slopes across agencies is precisely why including both LOS and AGY as separate predictors improves model fit — and why an interaction term LOS:AGY would allow the model to capture these differing growth rates directly rather than averaging across them.

Row

Predicted vs. Actual by Agency

Salary by Education Level

Row

Predicted vs. Actual — Interpretation

🎻

Paired violins compare actual (blue) and predicted (green) salary distributions per agency. A narrower predicted violin indicates the model compresses extreme values — capturing the centre but missing the tails, a known limitation of OLS minimising squared error. This compression is most visible for the Navy (NV) and Air Force (AF), where a subset of highly compensated civilian specialists pulls the actual distribution into a long upper tail that the model systematically underestimates. For DoD Other (DD), the predicted and actual violins align more closely, suggesting the models predictors do a better job covering the more administratively uniform roles that dominate that component.

Education Level — Interpretation

🎓

The monotonically increasing trend confirms education is a meaningful positive predictor of federal salary across all DoD components. The steeper rise at higher EDLVL codes suggests non-linear returns to education — a pattern particularly relevant for the Air Force and Navy, whose civilian workforces skew toward advanced degree holders in engineering, science, and acquisition fields. The jump at the top codes also reflects that doctoral and professional degree holders are disproportionately placed in senior research or senior executive positions. A quadratic term or factor encoding of EDLVL would allow the model to capture this accelerating return rather than imposing a single linear slope across all qualification levels.

Contract Spend

Column

Select Agency

Click an agency to filter both charts and their interpretations.

Column

Contract Spend

📋

Each panel shows the monthly federal contract obligation for that DoD component. The loess trend line (dotted green) smooths over month-to-month volatility to surface directional changes. End-of-fiscal-year spikes (September) are visible across all four agencies as remaining appropriations are obligated before the budget year closes — this is a structural feature of federal budgeting, not an anomaly. Comparing across panels, the Navy carries the largest absolute obligation volume driven by shipbuilding contracts, while the Army shows the most consistent month-to-month baseline reflecting steady installation and logistics spend. The Air Force displays more episodic peaks tied to procurement cycles, and DoD Other is the smoothest series given the diversity of agencies and contract types it aggregates. Use the sidebar to isolate any single component for a closer look.

⚖️

Annual averages smooth out seasonal noise to expose multi-year trends across all four components simultaneously. Salary overhead (dashed red) and CPI-U inflation (dot-dash gold) are rescaled to each agency’s contract spend axis for visual comparison — the axis reflects contract dollars only. Across all components, contract spend has grown faster than inflation for most of the period, indicating real spending increases beyond cost-of-living pressures. The Navy shows the starkest divergence between contract spend and salary overhead, reflecting how capital-intensive shipbuilding is relative to its civilian headcount. The Army and DoD Other show the tightest coupling between salary and contract trends, consistent with workforces where personnel costs and contracted services scale together. The 2021–2022 inflation surge (gold spike) is visible in all panels and preceded a corresponding rise in contract obligations as material and labour costs fed through into awarded contract values. Use the sidebar to isolate a single component and examine these dynamics in detail.

📋

The Army shows pronounced end-of-fiscal-year spikes each September as installation and base operations contracts are obligated before appropriations expire. The loess trend reveals a gradual upward drift in obligation volume over the period, consistent with sustained investment in base modernisation and logistics support contracts.

⚖️

For the Army, contract spend (blue) has grown faster than both salary overhead (red) and inflation (gold) over most of the period, suggesting real increases in contracted services and construction beyond what cost-of-living alone would explain. The post-2020 period shows contract spend accelerating while salary growth remains relatively steady.

📋

Navy contract spend is characterised by large, irregular spikes reflecting major shipbuilding and maintenance awards. These are multi-year contracts obligated in single months, which creates the sharp vertical jumps visible in the monthly series. The loess trend captures the underlying growth trajectory beneath this volatility.

⚖️

The Navy shows the largest absolute contract values of any component, driven by shipbuilding. Salary overhead is a comparatively small fraction of total spend, highlighting how capital-intensive naval procurement is relative to its civilian workforce cost. Inflation tracking is particularly relevant here as material and labour costs in shipyards are highly sensitive to CPI movements.

📋

Air Force obligations show a more episodic pattern driven by aircraft procurement and depot maintenance cycles. Periods of elevated spend often correspond to major platform contracts. The trend line highlights a step-change increase in the later years of the series.

⚖️

Air Force contract spend tracks inflation closely in earlier years before diverging upward, indicating procurement activity that outpaced general price levels. Salary overhead grows steadily in parallel, reflecting stable civilian workforce sizing even as contracted workload expands.

📋

DoD Other aggregates a wide range of defence agencies including DISA, DLA, and MDA. The resulting spend profile is smoother than the service branches, reflecting the diversity of contract types. End-of-year spikes are still present but less extreme relative to the baseline monthly level.

⚖️

DoD Other shows the tightest coupling between salary overhead and contract spend, which makes sense given that many of these agencies are primarily service providers where personnel costs and contracted services grow together. Periods where contract spend dips below the salary trend may reflect in-sourcing decisions or budget reductions in specific programmes.
